perturbation.original_text:
Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement for any reason, Eutectix shall have the option to purchase some or all of the Licensed Equipment at fair market value, less any amounts owed to Eutectix by Liquidmetal, except the equipment listed under Section 2.1(b) above. The following additional terms shall apply to Eutectix's exercise of this option:
(a) Eutectix's option hereunder shall be exercisable by providing Liquidmetal with written notice of its intention to exercise its chosen option no later than the effective date of termination. Such notice shall include a description of the assets Eutectix will purchase (the "Optioned Assets").
(b) In the event that Eutectix and Liquidmetal cannot agree to a fair market value for the Optioned Assets, then the fair market value shall be determined by an independent third-party appraisal. Eutectix and Liquidmetal shall each select one independent, qualified appraiser, and the two so selected shall select a third appraiser, all three to independently from one another determine the fair market value of the Optioned Assets. The purchase price shall be the mean of the fair market values as determined by the three appraisers.
perturbation.changed_text:
Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement for any reason, Eutectix shall have the option to purchase some or all of the Licensed Equipment at book value, less any amounts owed to Eutectix by Liquidmetal, except the equipment listed under Section 2.1(b) above. The following additional terms shall apply to Eutectix's exercise of this option:
(a) Eutectix's option hereunder shall be exercisable by providing Liquidmetal with written notice of its intention to exercise its chosen option no later than the effective date of termination. Such notice shall include a description of the assets Eutectix will purchase (the "Optioned Assets").
(b) In the event that Eutectix and Liquidmetal cannot agree to a book value for the Optioned Assets, then the book value shall be determined by an independent third-party appraisal. Eutectix and Liquidmetal shall each select one independent, qualified appraiser, and the two so selected shall select a third appraiser, all three to independently from one another determine the book value of the Optioned Assets. The purchase price shall be the mean of the book values as determined by the three appraisers.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'fair market value' to 'book value' introduces ambiguity. 'Book value' has a specific accounting definition, but its application to used equipment, particularly in a forced sale scenario, is unclear. The appraisal process determining book value adds more confusion because appraisals are more suited for 'fair market value' estimation, not 'book value'.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 9-626 determines deficiency or surplus in cases of collateral disposition after default. Using 'book value' instead of 'fair market value' might lead to an unfair calculation of deficiency or surplus because book value rarely reflects the actual worth of used equipment. This change could violate the principle of commercial reasonableness required in collateral disposition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 9-626. ACTION IN WHICH DEFICIENCY OR SURPLUS IS IN ISSUE. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 9-626. ACTION IN WHICH DEFICIENCY OR SURPLUS IS IN ISSUE. (a) [Applicable rules if amount of deficiency or surplus in issue.] In an action arising from a transaction, other than a consumer transaction , in which the amount of a deficiency or surplus is in issue, the following rules apply: (1) A secured party need not prove compliance with the provisions of this part relating to collection, enforcement, disposition, or acceptance unless the debtor or a secondary obligor places the secured party's compliance in issue. (2) If the secured party's compliance is placed in issue, the secured party has the burden of establishing that the collection, enforcement, disposition, or acceptance was conducted in accordance with this part. (3) Except as otherwise provided in Section 9-628 , if a secured party fails to prove that the collection, enforcement, disposition, or acceptance was conducted in accordance with the provisions of this part relating to collection, enforcement, disposition, or acceptance, the liability of a debtor or a secondary obligor for a deficiency is limited to an amount by which the sum of the secured obligation, expenses, and attorney's fees exceeds the greater of: (A) the proceeds of the collection, enforcement, disposition, or acceptance; or (B) the amount of proceeds that would have been realized had the noncomplying secured party proceeded in accordance with the provisions of this part relating to collection, enforcement, disposition, or acceptance. (4) For purposes of paragraph (3)(B), the amount of proceeds that would have been realized is equal to the sum of the secured obligation, expenses, and attorney's fees unless the secured party proves that the amount is less than that sum. (5) If a deficiency or surplus is calculated under Section 9-615(f) , the debtor or obligor has th
perturbation.justification: The change from 'fair market value' to 'book value' creates a legal contradiction because it may lead to an unfair calculation of deficiency or surplus after collateral disposition, potentially violating the principle of commercial reasonableness as suggested by the Law Explanation. UCC 9-626, as quoted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, addresses deficiency or surplus issues in transactions, stating that a secured party must prove compliance with enforcement and disposition procedures. Using 'book value', which often doesn't reflect actual worth, could be challenged as not commercially reasonable, potentially limiting the debtor's liability as described in subsection (3) if the secured party fails to prove compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Eutectix will provide facility space for the Licensed Equipment in a Eutectix-owned or leased secure and protected property that is restricted from unauthorized access or viewing (the "Eutectix Property"). Eutectix shall maintain the Licensed Equipment only in such designated Eutectix Property and shall not relocate the Licensed Equipment from such Eutectix Property without the prior written consent of Liquidmetal. Once Eutectix receives the Licensed Equipment, Eutectix will acknowledge in writing the receipt thereof. Eutectix's acknowledgement of receipt of the Licensed Equipment will constitute an acknowledgement that Eutectix has received and accepted and possesses the Licensed Equipment on bailment for the benefit of Liquidmetal and that such receipt of the Licensed Equipment is not subject to the terms and conditions of any applicable laws pertaining to sales and/or secured transactions.
perturbation.changed_text: Eutectix will provide facility space for the Licensed Equipment in a Eutectix-owned or leased secure and protected property that is restricted from unauthorized access or viewing (the "Eutectix Property"). Eutectix shall maintain the Licensed Equipment only in such designated Eutectix Property and shall relocate the Licensed Equipment from such Eutectix Property at its discretion without the prior written consent of Liquidmetal. Once Eutectix receives the Licensed Equipment, Eutectix will acknowledge in writing the receipt thereof. Eutectix's acknowledgement of receipt of the Licensed Equipment will constitute an acknowledgement that Eutectix has received and accepted and possesses the Licensed Equipment on bailment for the benefit of Liquidmetal and that such receipt of the Licensed Equipment is not subject to the terms and conditions of any applicable laws pertaining to sales and/or secured transactions.
perturbation.explanation: The change removing the requirement of Liquidmetal's consent for relocation directly contradicts the definition and conditions of bailment. In bailment, the bailee (Eutectix) has a duty of care and control over the property. Allowing Eutectix to relocate the equipment at will undermines Liquidmetal's ownership rights and the very essence of bailment, where control and care are paramount.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this is a bailment, the UCC 2A-103 regarding leases provides useful context and is generally applicable as persuasive authority to bailments. The secured party has rights in the leased goods. Permitting unrestrained relocation diminishes the bailor's/secured party's control over the asset, affecting their rights and potentially violating their interest in the bailed property.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2A-103. DEFINITIONS AND INDEX OF DEFINITIONS. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2A-103. DEFINITIONS AND INDEX OF DEFINITIONS. (1) In this Article unless the context otherwise requires: (a) " Buyer in ordinary course of business " means a person who in good faith and without knowledge that the sale to him [or her] is in violation of the ownership rights or security interest or leasehold interest of a third party in the goods , buys in ordinary course from a person in the business of selling goods of that kind but does not include a pawnbroker. "Buying" may be for cash or by exchange of other property or on secured or unsecured credit and includes receiving goods or documents of title under a pre-existing contract for sale but does not include a transfer in bulk or as security for or in total or partial satisfaction of a money debt. (b) " Cancellation " occurs when either party puts an end to the lease contract for default by the other party. (c) " Commercial unit " means such a unit of goods as by commercial usage is a single whole for purposes of lease and division of which materially impairs its character or value on the market or in use. A commercial unit may be a single article, as a machine, or a set of articles, as a suite of furniture or a line of machinery, or a quantity, as a gross or carload, or any other unit treated in use or in the relevant market as a single whole. (d) " Conforming " goods or performance under a lease contract means goods or performance that are in accordance with the obligations under the lease contract. (e) " Consumer lease " means a lease that a lessor regularly engaged in the business of leasing or selling makes to a lessee who is an individual and who takes under the lease primarily for a personal, family, or household purpose [, if the total payments to be made under the lease contract , excluding payments for options to renew or buy, do not exceed $_
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows Eutectix to relocate the licensed equipment at its discretion without Liquidmetal's consent, directly contradicting the principles of bailment described in the Perturbation Explanation and the UCC (as referenced in the Law Explanation). While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on lease definitions and 'buyer in ordinary course of business', the Law Explanation uses UCC 2A-103 regarding leases as persuasive authority. The ability to relocate the equipment at will undermines the bailor's control over the asset and potentially violates their interest in the bailed property, establishing a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, a "Force Majeure Event" shall mean the occurrence of unforeseen circumstances beyond a Party's control and without such Party's negligence or intentional misconduct, including, but not limited to, any act by any governmental authority, act of war, natural disaster, strike, boycott, embargo, shortage, riot, lockout, labor dispute, and civil commotion.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, a "Force Majeure Event" shall mean any circumstance preventing a Party's performance, including but not limited to, acts by any governmental authority, act of war, natural disaster, strike, boycott, embargo, shortage, riot, lockout, labor dispute, civil commotion, and routine economic hardship.
perturbation.explanation: By including "routine economic hardship" within the definition of Force Majeure, the modification significantly broadens the scope of events excusing performance. This is problematic because routine economic difficulties are generally foreseeable and are not considered valid reasons to avoid contractual obligations. This conflicts with the typical understanding of Force Majeure as extraordinary, unforeseeable events.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-615 excuses a seller's delay in delivery or non-delivery under specific circumstances. 'Economic hardship' is generally not unforeseeable and thus wouldn't excuse performance under this section. Adding it to the definition of Force Majeure would give a party an easy out, contradicting the intent of the UCC and general contract law, which requires genuine unforeseen circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. Except so far as a seller may have assumed a greater obligation and subject to the preceding section on substituted performance: (a) Delay in delivery or non-delivery in whole or in part by a seller who complies with paragraphs (b) and (c) is not a breach of his duty under a contract for sale if performance as agreed has been made impracticable by the occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made or by compliance in good faith with any applicable foreign or domestic governmental regulation or order whether or not it later proves to be invalid. (b) Where the causes mentioned in paragraph (a) affect only a part of the seller's capacity to perform, he must allocate production and deliveries among his customers but may at his option include regular customers not then under contract as well as his own requirements for further manufacture. He may so allocate in any manner which is fair and reasonable. (c) The seller must notify the buyer seasonably that there will be delay or non-delivery and, when allocation is required under paragraph (b), of the estimated quota thus made available for the buyer. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The inclusion of 'routine economic hardship' in the definition of 'Force Majeure Event' contradicts the established legal understanding and application of Force Majeure, as highlighted by UCC 2-615. Force Majeure is intended for unforeseen circumstances, not routine economic issues. The modified contract language weakens the requirement of unforeseen events by broadening the definition to include foreseeable hardships, which undermines the intent of UCC 2-615 which excuses performance only when it 'has been made impracticable by the occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made.' Routine economic hardship is generally foreseeable and therefore not a valid reason to avoid contractual obligations.
